coredata.dc:title: Seeking eye protection from biomass: Carbon dot-based optical blocking films with adjustable levels of blue light blocking
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Youqi
Huang, Xiaolin
Liu, Jiaheng
Ni, Jiaxin
Bai, Yibing
Zhao, Bin
Han, Shiyan
Zhang, Chunlei
coredata.dc:description: The intensity of blue light in white light emitting diodes is typically higher than that of the green and red-light components in screen displays and lighting systems. To reduce the potential harm of in white light emitting diodes to the eyes, in this paper, we have used microcrystalline cellulose to synthesize biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CD), which not only absorb short wavelength light to produce longer wavelength emissions, but also show concentration-dependent maximum excitation and maximum emission. The Bio-CDs were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to produce optical blocking films (OBF) that preferentially block blue light. OBFs have good transparency and also block blue light effectively. With OBFs containing 9.9% of Bio-CDs, the film blocked 99.6% and 98.6% of 395 nm light and 450 nm light respectively, and also blocked 93.4% and 97%, respectively, of the blue light emitted by computers and mobile phone screens. OBFs containing more than 9.9% Bio-CDs block blue light more than commercially available blue light blocking glasses. By adjusting the amount of Bio-CDs in the OBFs, it is possible to produce films with different degrees of blue light blocking to meet the requirements of different applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CDs)
Optical blocking film (OBF)
Adjustable degree with blue light blocking
High transparency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of optical blocking film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and high resolution TEM (inset) image of Bio-CDs; (b) Particle size histogram of Bio-CDs; (c) XPS of Bio-CDs; (d) FT-IR spectrum of Bio-CDs.

(a) UVabsorption spectra of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs with different concentrations (0.005–0.04 mg/mL); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs with different concentrations (0.01–0.1 mg/mL) (Ex = 365 nm); (c) Fluorescence lifetime curves of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs (0.01 mg/mL and 0.1 mg/mL); (d) Relative fluorescence intensities of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs containing 0–90% glycerol (Ex = 365 nm); (e) Fluorescence intensity of Bio-CDs on repeated cycling between 25 and 70 °C; (f) PL intensity of OBF (6.8% Bio-CDs) with continuous excitation at 365 nm for 50 min; (g) Normalized PL spectra of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs with different concentrations (0.01–4.7 mg/mL) (Ex = 365 nm); (h) Maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs with different concentrations (insets: fluorescence color images of solutions when illuminated by maximum excitation light source); (i) Schematic illustration showing mechanism of fluorescence emission of Bio-CDs.

(a) Digital photographsof letters “NEFU” (top) and color pictures (center) taken through OBFs (0–18% Bio-CDs) under sunlight, and fluorescent colors of OBFs (0–18% Bio-CDs) under UV light (365 nm) (bottom); (b) Field emission SEM images of Bio-CDs (inset: images showing the thickness of the OBFs); (c) Transmittance of OBFs (0–18% Bio-CDs); (d-f) CIE chromaticity diagrams of OBFs (0.4–18% Bio-CDs)excited at 365 nm, 405 nm and 450 nm, respectively; (g) PL intensity of OBFs with continuous excitation at 365 nm for 50 min; (h) UV (395 nm) light blocking ability of OBFs containing different amounts of Bio-CDs; (i) Blue (450 nm) light blocking ability of OBFs containing different amounts of Bio-CDs; (j) Digital photographs of LEDs obtained by encapsulation of 395 nm chip in different OBFs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV blocking by OBFs containing different proportions of Bio-CDs and commercially available glasses (G1, G2); (b) Blocking of blue light from computer screen by OBFs containing different proportions of Bio-CDs and commercially available glasses (G1, G2); (c) Blocking of blue light from mobile phone screen by OBFs containing different proportions of Bio-CDs and commercially available glasses (G1, G2); (d-f) CIE chromaticity coordinates of OBFs containing different proportions of Bio-CDs blocking blue light from computer, mobile phone and WLED; (g) Images of computer text viewed through single (top) and double (bottom) layers of OBF (9.9% Bio-CDs); (h) Images of “bamboo” viewed through commercially available anti-blue light glasses (G1, G2) and OBFs (6.8% and 9.9% Bio-CDs) (top) and photographs of objects taken through commercially available anti-blue light glasses (G1, G2) and OBFs (6.8% and 9.9% Bio-CDs) (bottom). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of (a) preparation of Bio-CDs and OBF and (b) blocking of blue light by OBF.

head.ce:title: Seeking eye protection from biomass: Carbon dot-based optical blocking films with adjustable levels of blue light blocking

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optical blocking films (OBFs) with high transparency and adjustable levels of blue light blocking, which can effectively protect the eyes from harmful blue light, were obtained using the light absorption and conversion properties of biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CDs)

The intensity of blue light in white light emitting diodes is typically higher than that of the green and red-light components in screen displays and lighting systems. To reduce the potential harm of in white light emitting diodes to the eyes, in this paper, we have used microcrystalline cellulose to synthesize biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CD), which not only absorb short wavelength light to produce longer wavelength emissions, but also show concentration-dependent maximum excitation and maximum emission. The Bio-CDs were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to produce optical blocking films (OBF) that preferentially block blue light. OBFs have good transparency and also block blue light effectively. With OBFs containing 9.9% of Bio-CDs, the film blocked 99.6% and 98.6% of 395 nm light and 450 nm light respectively, and also blocked 93.4% and 97%, respectively, of the blue light emitted by computers and mobile phone screens. OBFs containing more than 9.9% Bio-CDs block blue light more than commercially available blue light blocking glasses. By adjusting the amount of Bio-CDs in the OBFs, it is possible to produce films with different degrees of blue light blocking to meet the requirements of different applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Adjustable degree with blue light blocking

ce:section.ce:para: Electronic devices such as computers and mobile phones, are now indispensable in modern life. The screen displays and lighting systems of such devices are typically white light-emitting diodes (WLED), which offer high efficiency and energy savings. It can be seen from the color distribution and intensity of different WLEDs (Table S1 in Supplementary Information) that most light sources emit cool white light as a result of the high intensity of blue light. Long-term exposure of the eyes to the blue light has been reported to cause eye strain, as well as retinal damage [1–3], and can lead to macular degeneration, and photodynamic damage to aging eye lenses [4,5]. Blue light and ultraviolet cut-off filters (blue filters) [6], blue light attenuating intraocular lenses [6], antioxidants [7] and blue light blocking films [3] have all been investigated for their ability to reduce the retinal damage caused by blue light. Despite of these great efforts, alternative techniques that are independent of the incident angle of the light and use optical absorption and conversion to block the eye-damaging effects of blue light are being actively investigated. Photoluminescent materials that have light absorption and conversion properties typically include rare earths [8–10], semiconductor quantum dots [11] and carbon dots [12–16]. The use of rare earth metals is limited by their expense and lack of renewability and that of semiconductor quantum dots, which often contain cadmium, is limited by their toxicity. Carbon dots, on the other hand, are easily prepared, are sustainable and have tunable optical properties [17,18]. Among many raw materials that can be used to prepare carbon dots, biomass and its derived resources have attracted considerable momentum [19–20] in recent years as they are abundant, non-toxic and renewable [21–24]. Although biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CDs) are widely used in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting applications [25], cell imaging [26] and WLEDs [27], their use to protect eyes from the damaging effects of blue light is still rarely described. Carbon dot films currently used for blocking blue light often have low blocking efficiency [28], poor transparency [29–31], and high carbon dot consumption or they usenon-renewable raw materials [3], compromising their practical application.

To overcome these problems, and to broaden the application of Bio-CDs in the blue light blocking field, we used sustainable biomass microcrystalline cellulose and ethylenediamine to prepare Bio-CDs, with adjustable absorption and conversion wavelengths. These Bio-CDs, which act as blue light blocking units, were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare an optical blocking film (OBF) as shown in (Scheme 1
a). By adjusting the proportion of Bio-CDs in the film, we were able to obtain OBFs with high transparency and different degrees of blue light blocking. Through light absorption and conversion, the OBFs can effectively block blue light emitted by WLEDs in computers, mobile phone screens and lighting systems, as well as ultraviolet rays emitted by the sun (Scheme 1 b). The OBFs are superior to previously reported carbon dot films in terms of the choice of raw material, the extent of blue light blocking and transparency. Significantly, OBFs also have better blocking ability than commercially available blue light blocking glasses.

Microcrystalline cellulose and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (MW1750, 99%) were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China) and ethylenediamine (99%, AR) was purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All purchased chemicals were used without further purification. Deionized water was produced using a Clever-Q30 UT ultrapure water system (Zhiang Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). Ultraviolet (UV) and blue lightemittingdiodes (LED) (395 nm and 450 nm) were purchased from Looking Long Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China).

The Bio-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal approach. Microcrystalline cellulose (0.2 g) and deionized water (70 mL) were placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and ethylenediamine (0.5 mL) was added to the solution, which was then stirred thoroughly using a glass rod. The mixed solution was heated to 200 ℃ for 5 h. After being allowed to cool to room temperature (rt), the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm micro-filtration membrane. The filtrate was freeze dried to provide the Bio-CDs as a sticky solid.

Nine different films, containing 0, 0.4, 1.8, 3.5, 6.8, 9.9, 12.8, 15.5 or 18% Bio-CDs were prepared by adding an appropriate amount of an aqueous solution of Bio-CDs (11 mg/mL) to a mixture of PVA (3 g) and deionized water (80 mL) in a glass beaker. The solutions were stirred in a constant temperature water bath at 95 °C for 1 h and then ultrasonicated for 1 h. The solutions of Bio-CDs were then poured into clean glass tanks (20 cm × 30 cm) and allowed to dry at rt to form the OBFs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., New York, USA). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Fourier FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). Photoluminescence measurements were made using an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc.) and fluorescence decay curves were constructed with a QM8000 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., Edison, NJ, USA). LED parameters were recorded using an OHSP350 spectrometer (Hongpu Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, China). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were collected by an Apreo S scanning electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1950 ultraviolet–visible spectrofluorometer (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using an X’Pert3 Powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., Malvern, UK).

Biomass based carbon dots (Bio-CD) were prepared using a hydrothermal method by heating an aqueous solution of microcrystalline cellulose and ethylenediamine at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm micro-filtration membrane to remove undissolved residues, and the filtrate was then freeze dried to provide Bio-CDs. The as-prepared Bio-CDs displayed good dispersity (Fig. 1
a), with a mean diameter of 4.2 nm and particle diameter distribution in the range 2–9 nm (Fig. 1 b).High resolution TEM images of the Bio-CDs showed well-resolved lattice spacings of 0.22 nm (Fig. 1 a, inset), which correspond to the [100] crystal facet of graphitic carbon [32,33]. The XRD spectrum had a broad peak at ∼ 20.78° ( Fig. S1 ), which was attributed to an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the [002] planes of graphitic carbon [34–35]. The full XPS spectrum indicated that the Bio-CDs contain elemental C (69.22%), O (13.96%) and N (16.82%) (Fig. 1 c). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum showed peaks corresponding to CC/CC at 284.57 eV, to CN/CO at 285.68 eV and to CO/CN at 287.6 eV [36–39] ( Fig. S2
a). The O 1 s spectrum showed a peak at 531.18 eV corresponding to CO and a peak at 532.49 eV corresponding to CO [3,38,40–41] ( Fig. S2
b). The N 1 s spectrum showed peaks at 399.49 eV and 401.2 eV, which were assigned to CN [42] and CN [43,44], respectively ( Fig. S2
c). The FT-IR spectrum showed peaks corresponding to the –NH/–OH, CO/CN [42,45–48], CN and CO moieties [17,49–51] (Fig. 1 d), confirming the presence of O- and N-rich groups on the surface of the carbon core. The presence of these groups leads to diversification of the surface state, which facilitates long wavelength emission.

We also studied the effect of viscosity andtemperature on the intensity of emission of the Bio-CDs. As the proportion of glycerol in the solution was increased, the intensity of emission of the solution increased continuously (Fig. 2 d and S5) since aggregation of groups on the carbon core is promoted in more viscous solutions [59]. Reversible converted fluorescence was observed over several heating and cooling cycles, indicating good thermal stability of the Bio-CDs. The decrease in fluorescence with increasing temperature is due to the effect of nonradiative transitions [42,55] (Fig. 2 e and S6). These results show that Bio-CDs have strong fluorescence emission and good capacity for photoexcitation conversion, and also that the emission intensity can be affected by temperature and viscosity. After continuous excitation with 365 nm UV light for 50 min, the fluorescence of an aqueous solution of Bio-CDs decreased by less than 5% (Fig. 2 f), confirming that Bio-CDs have good photostability. When used as a fluorescent printing ink, a solution of Bio-CDs showed strong blue fluorescence images under 365 nm UV light but was colorless under natural light ( Fig. S7 ).

We next investigated the luminescence mechanism of the Bio-CDs. In dilute solution (0.01–0.1 mg/mL), the PL of Bio-CDs increased with increasing concentration (Fig. 2 b). When the concentration of the Bio-CDs exceeded 0.1 mg/mL, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of aqueous solutions of Bio-CDs were red-shifted (Fig. 2 g, 2 h) and the fluorescence color changed from blue to orange (Fig. 2 h inset), consistent with the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage(CIE) coordinates ( Fig. S8 ). We propose the following mechanism for this interesting fluorescence emission. When electrons in the ground state absorb photons and undergo a HOMO → LUMO transition to the excited state, they may revert to another excited state with a lower vibrational energy level through internal conversion and vibration relaxation, and then emit fluorescence after the electrons return to the ground state (Fig. 2 i). Solutions containing low concentrations (0.01–0.1 mg/mL) of Bio-CDs emit blue fluorescence, which was assigned to the HOMO(π) → LUMO(π*) transition of CC bonds in the sp2 C domain. Higher concentrations of Bio-CDs (0.1–4.7 mg/mL) promote formation of aggregates of O- and N-rich groups on the carbon cores, generating a surface state with new energy levels, with introduction of nC=O and nC=N into the electronic structures [42,60,61]. With increases in concentration, the UV absorption of solutions of Bio-CDs shifted to longer wavelengths which promotes the n-π* transition of the new energy level. The red-shift in fluorescence of more concentrated solutions of Bio-CDs (0.1–4.7 mg/mL) can thus be largely attributed to the altered surface state. The electrons can transition from the HOMO (nO) and HOMO (nN) to the LUMO(π*) and, at the same time, the excited electrons will be deactivated by radiative recombination [42,58,60], resulting in long wavelength emission. Specifically, at low concentrations, the sp2 carbon region state of the carbon core dominates and blue photons are emitted, whereas at higher concentrations, aggregates on the surface of the carbon core gradually become dominant, resulting in long wavelength fluorescence emission [55,56,58,62] (Fig. 2 i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of optical blocking film
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration-dependent tunability of the maximum emission wavelength suggests that Bio-CDs are ideally suited to applications that specifically require blocking of blue light. OBFs containing different amounts of Bio-CDs (0–18% Bio-CDs) were prepared by mixing Bio-CDs with PVA. The OBFs are colorless to brown under natural light, depending on the loading of incorporated Bio-CDs (Fig. 3
a top), when observed through the OBFs, the letters “NEFU” (Fig. 3 a top) and the color pictures (Fig. 3 a center) were barely affected by the color of the OBFs themselves. SEM images showed that the OBFs have a smooth and flat morphology (Fig. 3 b). Despite increasing amounts of Bio-CDs, the OBFs had similar thicknesses (about 70–75 μm) and their surfaces remained smooth and flat (Fig. 3 b inset). Cross-sectional SEM images confirmed that the Bio-CDs were uniformly dispersed in the OBFs.

Next, the optical properties of the OBFs were studied. The transmittance of pure PVA film in the blue region of the spectrum (less than450 nm) is ∼ 90% (Fig. 3 c), but as the amount of Bio-CDs in the PVA was increased, the transmittance at ≤ 450 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 3 c) and the absorbance increased ( Fig. S9 ), showing that OBFs strongly absorb light in the UV to blue region of the spectrum. The OBFs (0–18% Bio-CDs) showed blue to yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3 a bottom), consistent with the CIE coordinates (Fig. 3 d). The changes in fluorescence color caused by increased concentrations of Bio-CDs in the PVA, which promotes the formation of aggregated groups on the surface of the carbon core, are due to the generation of surface states with new energy levels. Since electron transitions are dominated by the surface states, this results in a red shift in the fluorescence emission [58,63]. The mechanism underlying this red shift in fluorescence emission is similar to that underlying the red shift in fluorescence emission of Bio-CDs in solution.

To investigate the light conversion capability of the OBFs, we measured the emission spectra of OBFs with excitation at 400 nm and 450 nm ( Fig. S10 ). As the amount of Bio-CDs in the OBF increases, the OBF absorbs photons more efficiently and converts them into longer wavelength emission (green light to red light); the CIE coordinates are consistent with the color changes (Fig. 3 e, 3f). OBFs can thus absorb short wavelength photons (≤450 nm) and convert them into longer wavelength light (green to red light), thus protecting the eyes from blue light. The OBFs also have adjustable light conversion properties. The emission intensity of OBF (6.8% Bio-CDs) decreased by only 2.2% after continuous excitation at 365 nm for 50 min, confirming that the OBF has good photostability (Fig. 3 g).

In order to further study the light absorption and conversion ability of OBFs, we measured the intensity of the emission spectra of OBFs (0–18% Bio-CDs) when used to block light from 395 nm and 450 nm LEDs. Pure PVA film has almost no blocking effect on 395 nm and 450 nm light but its optical blocking was gradually enhanced as the proportion of Bio-CDs in the film increased (Fig. 3 h, 3i and S11). When the content of Bio-CDs in OBF reaches 9.9%, the light blocking rates of 395 nm and 450 nm are 99.6% and 98.6%, respectively (Fig. 3 h, 3i). As the proportion of Bio-CDs increased further, both 395 nm and 450 nm light became completely blocked. Because of the light conversion properties of the OBFs, encapsulation the OBFs on the 395 nm chip using epoxy resin resulted in purple → white → orange LEDs (Fig. 3 j). The CIE chromaticity coordinates showed consistent color variation ( Fig. S12 ). A WLED with CIE chromaticity coordinates (CCR) of (0.2950, 0.3214), correlation color temperature (CCT) of 6653 K and color rendering index (CRI) of 81 was obtained by encapsulating OBF (6.8% Bio-CDs) on a 395 nm chip ( Fig. S12 inset). Encapsulation of the 450 nm chip with OBF (18% Bio-CDs) produced a warm WLED with CCR of (0.3781, 0.3499), CCT of 3842 K and CRI of 85 ( Fig. S13
a). The electroluminescence spectrum is shown in Fig. S13
b. The warm WLED can render the true color of objects ( Fig. S14 ). These results all confirm the ability of OBFs to absorb short wavelength light and convert it to long wavelength emission.

To investigate practical applications of the ability of OBFs to block ultraviolet and blue light, we first measured the intensity spectrum of OBFs when used to block UV light emitted by the sun. As the proportion of Bio-CDs in the film increased, the intensity of ≤ 400 nm light transmitted through the film gradually decreased ( Fig. S15 ) and the extent of blocking increased (Fig. 4
a). When the proportion of Bio-CDs in the OBF exceeded 6.8%, the film completely blocked solar UV light (Fig. 4 a inset). Analysis showed that the extent of blocking of UV light from solar by OBF (6.8% Bio-CDs) was comparable to blocking by G2 (Fig. 4 a inset). As a demonstration of a real application, we investigated the ability of OBFs to protect wood against UV aging. Part of a wooden block was covered with OBFs containing different proportions of Bio-CDs ( Fig. S16 top) and the blocks were then irradiated with a 340 nm (80 W) UV lamp for 60 h. The wooden blocks covered by pure PVA film became significantly darker, showing that pure PVA film does not protect the wood from UV aging ( Fig. S16 bottom). Wooden blocks covered by OBFs, on the other hand, were protected from discoloration, with films containing higher proportions of Bio-CDs providing greater protection.

As a demonstration of another practical application, we investigated the ability of OBFs to block the blue light emitted by computer and mobile phone screens. The intensity spectrum of light filtered through different OBFs was compared with the intensity spectra of light filtered through commercially available blue light blocking glasses with 85% (G1) and 95% (G2) efficiency ( Figs. S17 and S18 ). As the proportion of Bio-CDs in the film increased, the ability of the OBF to block the blue light emitted by computers and mobile phone screens was enhanced (Fig. 4 b, 4c), and the proportion of transmitted blue light gradually decreased (Tables S2 and S3). These results are consistent with the changes in intensity of blue, green and red light in the intensity spectra ( Figs. S17 and S18 ). The CIE coordinates of light from computer and mobile phone screens changed from white to orange after passing through OBFs (Fig. 4 d, 4e), and the color temperature changed from cold white to warm white (Tables S2 and S3). OBFs can thus absorb blue light from computer and phone screens and emit green and red light that is harmless to the eyes. Further analysis showed that the extent of blocking of blue light from computer and mobile phone screens by OBF (6.8% Bio-CDs) was comparable to blocking by G1 and that blocking by OBF (9.9% Bio-CDs) was comparable to blocking by G2 (Fig. 4 b inset, 4c inset). When the proportion of Bio-CDs in the OBFs exceeded 9.9%, blue light blocking by the film was significantly higher than by G1 or G2, indicating that blue light blocking by OBFs can be regulated by controlling the proportion of Bio-CDs.

We measured the ability of OBFs, G1 and G2 to block blue light using a commercially available anti-blue light test card. Video 1 shows that OBFs (6.8% and 9.9% Bio-CDs) significantly block blue light and that these films are as effective as the commercially available anti-blue light glasses, G1 and G2. Wealsotested the effect of different OBFs on the three colors (blue, green and red) of a WLED.
Video 2 allows direct observation of the gradual decrease in blue light intensity, with a concomitant increase in the intensity of green and red light. The CIE coordinates changed from white to orange-red (Fig. 4 f), further confirming the ability of OBFs to absorb blue light and convert it into green and red light.

We next analyzed the transparency of the OBFs. The presence of OBF coverson computer and mobile phone screens did not affect the readability of the screen images ( Fig. S19 ); even two OBFs used together did not affect the clarity of the images (Fig. 4 g and S20). Objects and landscapes viewed and photographed through OBFs were also clear and transparent ( Fig. S21 ), showing that OBFs are highly transparent and that incorporation of Bio-CDs does not affect the transparency of the film. To further demonstrate the high transparency of OBFs, we compared OBFs (6.8% and 9.9% Bio-CDs) with anti-blue light glasses (G1 and G2) for the observation of images and real objects. For both the close observation of a “bamboo” diagram and the observation of a real object from a distance, the transparency of OBFs was comparable to that of G1 and G2 (Fig. 4 h). Compared with other blocking films that have been described [3,28–31], OBFs not only have good transparency but also block blue light more effectively (Table S4). These results all confirm that OBFs with adjustable blue light blocking and high transparency can be used as anti-blue light films to protect human eyes from blue light. Importantly, since Bio-CDs show good blue light absorption and conversion,they have many potential applications where blue light blocking is required.

In summary, the Bio-CDs with adjustable luminescence and stable optical properties, have been successfully prepared from the biomass resource microcrystalline cellulose using a hydrothermal method. The incorporation of Bio-CDs into PVA can produce blue light blocking OBFs. Compared with the published light blocking films [3,28–31], OBFs not only have good transparency but also block blue light more effectively. Furthermore, OBFs containing more than 9.9% Bio-CDs block blue light more than commercially available blue light blocking glasses. Importantly, OBFs with different levels of blue light blocking can be obtained by adjusting the proportion of Bio-CDs in the film, allowing for different requirements for blue light blocking. The research results demonstrate that films containing Bio-CDs have potential applications as blue light blocking component in blocking harmful blue light emitted by electronic products such as computers and mobile phones. The exploration of new applications of Bio-CDs in electronic devices are actively ongoing in our group.

Youqi Han: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaolin Huang: Validation, Formal analysis. Jiaheng Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Jiaxin Ni: Data curation. Yibing Bai: Investigation. Bin Zhao: Investigation. Shiyan Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Chunlei Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171715), the Hei Long Jiang Postdoctoral Foundation (LBH-Q21003) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572020BB07). The authors are grateful for the funding.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.02.115.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 53% Efficient Red Emissive Carbon Quantum Dots for High Color Rendering and Stable Warm White-Light-Emitting Diodes
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, F. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, H. Zhong, L. Fan, S. Yang, 53% Efficient Red Emissive Carbon Quantum Dots for High Color Rendering and Stable Warm White-Light-Emitting Diodes, Adv. Mater. 29(37) (2017). https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201702910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct blending of multicolor carbon quantum dots into fluorescent films for white light emitting diodes with an adjustable correlated color temperature
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ding, J. Zheng, J. Wang, Y. Yang, X. Liu, Direct blending of multicolor carbon quantum dots into fluorescent films for white light emitting diodes with an adjustable correlated color temperature, J. Mater. Chem. C 7(6) (2019) 1502-1509. https://doi.org/10.1039/c8tc04887h.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultraviolet to blue blocking and wavelength convertible films using carbon dots for interrupting eye damage caused by general lighting
Nano Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.X. Ba, L. Zhang, Y.L. Yin, F.L. Jiang, P. Jiang, Y. Liu, Luminescent carbon dots with concentration-dependent emission in solution and yellow emission in solid state, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 565 (2020) 77-85. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.01.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aggregation-induced emission: together we shine, united we soar!
Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Mei, N.L. Leung, R.T. Kwok, J.W. Lam, B.Z. Tang, Aggregation-Induced Emission: Together We Shine, United We Soar!, Chem. Rev. 115(21) (2015) 11718-940. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.chemrev.5b00263.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation-Independent Dual-Color Carbon Dots: Surface-State Controlling and Solid-State Lighting
ACS Photonics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Chen, H. Gao, X. Chen, G. Fang, S. Yuan, Y. Yuan, Excitation-Independent Dual-Color Carbon Dots: Surface-State Controlling and Solid-State Lighting, ACS Photonics 4(9) (2017) 2352-2358. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsphotonics.7b00675.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with concentration-modulated fluorescence: Aggregation-induced multicolor emission
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Su, Z. Xie, M. Zheng, Carbon dots with concentration-modulated fluorescence: Aggregation-induced multicolor emission, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 573 (2020) 241-249. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.04.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of self-quenching-resistant biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with high yield for white lighting emitting diodes
Dyes Pigm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ge, X. Huang, J. Ni, Y. Han, C. Zhang, S. Li, J. Cao, J. Li, Z. Chen, S. Han, One-step synthesis of self-quenching-resistant biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with high yield for white lighting emitting diodes, Dyes Pigm. 185 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophobic carbon dots with blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission
Nat. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, Y. Liu, Z. Guo, B. Lei, J. Zhuang, X. Zhang, Z. Liu, C. Hu, Hydrophobic carbon dots with blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission, Nat. Commun. 10(1) (2019) 1789. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-019-09830-6.
